title: UGC NET English (Paper 2) - 100 Question Model Test
description: UGC NET English Paper 2 - High-Probability Model Test (100 MCQs)

questions.question: The phrase “brave new world” in Shakespeare’s The Tempest is spoken by:
Which of the following is a one-act play by J.M. Synge?
options: The Playboy of the Western World

questions.question: The Restoration play The Country Wife was written by:
The concept of “rasa” in Sanskrit poetics is associated with:
A Streetcar Named Desire is a play by:
The critic who described Restoration comedy as “artificial” and “immoral” in a famous essay is:
Brecht’s The Caucasian Chalk Circle derives its central parable from:
options: A Chinese legend and the Judgment of Solomon

questions.question: The term “Machiavel” in Elizabethan drama refers to a character who is:
The poem “My Last Duchess” by Robert Browning is set in:
The line “To strive, to seek, to find, and not to yield” is from Tennyson’s:
The Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam was translated into English by:
The poem “A Far Cry from Africa” by Derek Walcott grapples with the conflict between:
John Milton’s Lycidas is a pastoral elegy written in memory of:
The poetic technique of using a word whose sound suggests its meaning (e.g., “buzz”, “hiss”) is called:
Which of the following is the correct sequence of medieval English poetry?
options: Beowulf, Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, Piers Plowman, The Canterbury Tales
Sir Gawain, Beowulf, Canterbury Tales, Piers Plowman
Beowulf, Piers Plowman, Sir Gawain, Canterbury Tales
Canterbury Tales, Beowulf, Sir Gawain, Piers Plowman

questions.question: The novel Robinson Crusoe is often considered an early example of:
Charles Dickens’ A Tale of Two Cities begins with the famous words:
options: “It was the best of times, it was the worst of times…”
“Last night I dreamt I went to Manderley again.”

questions.question: The novel One Hundred Years of Solitude by Gabriel García Márquez opens with the character:
options: José Arcadio Buendía facing a firing squad
Remedios the Beauty ascending to heaven

questions.question: Who among the following is the author of the graphic novel Watchmen?
The Golden Notebook by Doris Lessing is a pioneering work of:
The term “Künstlerroman” refers to a novel about:
options: The growth of an artist into maturity

questions.question: The short story “The Dead”, which concludes Joyce’s Dubliners, ends with:
options: The image of snow falling all over Ireland
A violent epiphany at a party

questions.question: The Narrow Road to the Deep North (2014 Man Booker winner) is by:
The essay “Tradition and the Individual Talent” argues that:
options: The poet’s personality should be expressed intensely
Poetry is an escape from emotion and personality
The individual poet is the sole genius
Tradition is an impediment to creativity

questions.question: Francis Bacon’s essays are known for their aphoristic style and present a worldly, utilitarian philosophy. His essay “Of Marriage and Single Life” famously begins:
options: “He that hath wife and children hath given hostages to fortune…”
“A single man is like a fly in a parlor…”

questions.question: The Social Contract (1762), which influenced the French Revolution, was written by:
The Autobiography of Malcolm X was written in collaboration with:
Thomas De Quincey’s On the Knocking at the Gate in Macbeth is a classic example of:
The collective term for the essays published by Joseph Addison and Richard Steele in The Tatler and The Spectator is:
Phonetically, the word “jumped” is transcribed as:
The linguistic phenomenon where the meaning of a word becomes more negative over time (e.g., Old English cnafa “boy” → Modern English knave “rogue”) is called:
In the IPA chart, the sound /j/ as in “yes” is a:
The sentence “Flying planes can be dangerous” demonstrates:
The most widely used method for second language teaching that emphasizes interaction as both the means and the goal is:
The relationship between the words “teacher” and “pupil” can best be described as:
The branch of linguistics that studies language in relation to society is called:
“Code-switching” in bilingual contexts means:
options: Alternating between two or more languages in a single conversation
The gradual loss of a first language
Creating a new language from contact

questions.question: The Wood’s Despatch of 1854 is significant in the history of English education in India because it:
options: Recommended the establishment of universities in Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras
Declared that English should be the only medium of instruction

questions.question: The novel Coolie by Mulk Raj Anand is a powerful portrayal of:
options: The exploitation of an orphan boy in colonial India

questions.question: Eunice de Souza is a significant Indian English poet known for her collections such as:
options: The Old Playhouse and Other Poems

questions.question: Salman Rushdie’s essay “Imaginary Homelands” deals primarily with:
options: The historical accuracy of the novel
The experience of exile and the creation of imaginary homelands in literature
A critique of Indian English poetry
The aesthetics of the graphic novel

questions.question: The Indian English poet who translated the devotional poetry of Lal Ded and Nund Rishi from Kashmiri is:
Who among the following is the author of The Great Indian Novel, a satirical retelling of the Mahabharata set against India’s independence movement?
The term “Englishes” (as in World Englishes) was popularized by:
The concept of “hegemony” is distinct from simple domination because it implies:
options: Consent of the governed achieved through ideology and culture

questions.question: The book There Ain’t No Black in the Union Jack by Paul Gilroy explores:
options: The history of slavery in America
Race, nation, and culture in Britain

questions.question: The term “cyborg”, central to Donna Haraway’s “A Cyborg Manifesto”, represents:
options: A hybrid of machine and organism that challenges rigid boundaries

questions.question: The literary critic who argued that the rise of the novel in the 18th century was closely tied to the rise of individualism and the middle class is:
Gayatri Chakravorty Spivak’s strategic essentialism refers to:
options: A belief in the fixed essence of all identities
The temporary use of identity-based categories for political purposes
A rejection of all identity politics
A purely philosophical concept with no political application

questions.question: The Location of Culture by Homi K. Bhabha introduces the concept of “mimicry” as:
options: An exact copy of the colonizer
A subversive imitation that reveals the ambivalence of colonial discourse
The native’s desire to erase colonial influence

questions.question: The critic who categorized Western theories of literature into four coordinates—universe, work, artist, audience—in The Mirror and the Lamp is:
The doctrine of “rasa” according to Bharata holds that the aesthetic pleasure arises from:
options: The combination of vibhavas (determinants), anubhavas (consequents), and vyabhichari bhavas (transitory feelings)
The moral lesson of the drama
The technical virtuosity of the performance

questions.question: Sidney’s An Apology for Poetry asserts that the poet “nothing affirms, and therefore never lieth.” This is because:
options: The poet creates a second nature and deals with the ideal, not the actual
Poetry is purely fictional and unrelated to truth

questions.question: T.S. Eliot’s term “objective correlative” first appears in his essay:
F.R. Leavis’s The Great Tradition excludes which of the following novelists from the “great tradition” of the English novel?
The Russian Formalist concept of “fabula” refers to:
options: The plot as arranged in the text
The chronological sequence of events in the story

questions.question: “Death of the Author” advocates that the meaning of a text is:
options: Created by the reader at the moment of reading

questions.question: Jacques Derrida’s critique of logocentrism aims to dismantle the Western philosophical preference for:
The term “phallogocentrism”, a blend of phallocentrism and logocentrism, is associated with:
Gender Trouble argues that gender is performative and is constituted through:
options: The repeated stylization of the body

questions.question: “Intertextuality”, the idea that a text is shaped by other texts, was coined by:
The Frankfurt School’s critique of the “culture industry” argues that mass culture:
options: Promotes conformity and distracts from capitalist exploitation
Is always aesthetically superior to high art

questions.question: According to Northrop Frye’s Anatomy of Criticism, the mythos of spring corresponds to:
The term “dialogism” in Bakhtin’s theory of the novel refers to:
options: The use of dialogue in drama
The multi-voiced, polyphonic nature of discourse
The absence of the author’s voice

questions.question: The term “dystopia” is best defined as:
options: A society characterized by human misery, oppression, and totalitarian control

questions.question: The Wretched of the Earth includes a preface by:
A bibliography that includes descriptive notes summarizing each source is called:
The process of establishing the authoritative text of a literary work by comparing various editions and manuscripts is known as:
A research design that studies a single case or a small number of cases in depth over a period of time is called:
In APA style, an in-text citation for a direct quotation must include:
“Open access” journals refer to scholarly journals that are:
options: Available online without financial or technical barriers

questions.question: The statement “Poetry is the criticism of life” was made by:
In Aristotle’s Poetics, the term “peripeteia” refers to:
options: The final scene of a tragedy

questions.question: The “green light” in The Great Gatsby symbolizes:
options: Gatsby’s hopes and the American Dream

questions.question: Who among the following wrote the verse-novel The Golden Gate?
The novel We Need New Names (2013) is by:
The line “O body swayed to music, O brightening glance, / How can we know the dancer from the dance?” ends Yeats’s poem:
Which of the following characters is NOT from Shakespeare’s Hamlet?
The poet John Keats died in:
The novel The Handmaid’s Tale by Margaret Atwood is a work of:
The Rape of the Lock is a mock-epic poem by:
The line “That’s my last Duchess painted on the wall, / Looking as if she were alive” introduces a dramatic monologue by:
The feminist slogan “The personal is political” is encapsulated in the essays of the 1960s and 70s by:
The term “Angst” in existentialist thought refers to:
options: Profound anxiety and dread in the face of freedom

questions.question: The play Who’s Afraid of Virginia Woolf? is by:
The collection of poems Leaves of Grass was first published in:
The novel Midnight’s Children employs the narrative device of:
options: A first-person narrator, Saleem Sinai, who tells his story to a companion
Multiple shifting narrators without a central voice

questions.question: The Canadian author Margaret Atwood also wrote:
The linguistic concept of “deep structure” and “surface structure” appears in:
The “implicature” theory, which accounts for what a speaker implies rather than literally says, was developed by:
The novel The Moor’s Last Sigh is written by:
The Home and the World (Ghare Baire) is a novel by:
In MLA style, the title of a book in the works cited list should be:
The concept of “negative capability” was defined by Keats in a letter to:
options: His brother George and Thomas Keats

questions.question: The narrative poem The Canterbury Tales is set in the framework of:
The author of The Color Purple (1982) is:
The term “Absurd” in the context of the Theatre of the Absurd was first used by:
The phrase “the willing suspension of disbelief” was originally proposed in relation to:
options: The acceptance of supernatural elements in poetry

questions.question: Which of the following Shakespeare plays is set primarily in Venice and Cyprus?
The novel A Fine Balance (1995), set during the Indian Emergency, is by:
